title: Kaji is The Bond Girl Not James Bond An In Depth Analysis
display_title: Kaji is The Bond Girl Not James Bond An In Depth Analysis
wikitext:
[]: Kaji is The Bond Girl, Not James Bond: An In-Depth Analysis             Quote Post by obliterates » Wed Jul 09, 2014 2:53 pm I figured I should make a separate post for this Analysis that's spreading on Tumblr, probably birthed right after the Asuka one since it references the same author. It's a Kaji character analysis called Kaji is The Bond Girl, Not James Bond: Emotional Disguises, Watermelons, and The Day The Earth Stood Still . The first part points out why the movie is influential to Kaji's character, and the rest explains why Kaji's role in the story is to deconstruct the James Bond trope and hide a true feminine personality he wants to keep secret from everyone else. There's also a link to another analysis within this one that explains the symbolic weight of the Adult Quartet; Misato, Kaji, Ritsuko, and Gendo. It's pretty interesting and features explanations that I've never heard before until now, like the importance of coffee to Kaji's character. Here's the link. http://fujiij.tumblr.com/post/88618003609/s-s A transcript under the Spoiler, but without the links to the other analysis. SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Jul 09, 2014 3:17 pm The coffee details were quite interesting, and something I never picked up on before, especially in that pot of coffee that gets spilled in EoE. Good stuff.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Wed Jul 09, 2014 3:38 pm Well done. This analysis brought up quite a few points and offered some perspectives that I'd never seen or considered. Kaji was always one of the most interesting characters to me, and this just made me respect him that much more.

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE » Wed Jul 09, 2014 4:42 pm Already read it a long time ago. Still a good analysis. I like how Kaji isn't confirmed to be dead, just like Klaatu. I posted it a few days ago in some threads.

[]:             Quote Post by obliterates » Thu Jul 10, 2014 7:35 pm SEELE wrote: I like how Kaji isn't confirmed to be dead, just like Klaatu.

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE-01 » Thu Jul 10, 2014 10:31 pm Very interesting... I did notice Misato's shift to coffee after Kaji's death, but I never noticed before that he is only seen drinking that...

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jul 10, 2014 11:23 pm Great read! And interestingly enough, PSP NGE2 implies Kaji caused the Nerv power outage (we see a cut of him saying his #13 line of continuing work) although the series itself never flat-out says anything...

[]:             Quote Post by Compiling_Autumn » Fri Jul 11, 2014 12:00 am This analysis makes some good, salient points, but I can't get over how silly calling Kaji a "Bond Girl" is, especially when you consider the actual Bond girls run the gamut from lovelorn sidekick to backstabbing villain. The phrase is just thrown out there to show that Kaji is not like James Bond (surprise, surprise). I'm willing to change my opinion if the next Bond movie has James confessing to his girl that he ran from her many years ago because she reminded him of his mother. Let the record state that I am open minded about this, but right now I'm pretty certain that the "Bond Girl" comparison is lazy.

[]:             Quote Post by goodnight humanitarian » Fri Jul 11, 2014 1:14 am I think this analysis makes really good points but I don't get why it had to be framed in the context of James Bond. Kaji is less of a specific deconstruction of James Bond and more of a general subversion of traditional masculinity and, along with Misato, gender relations. The James Bond relationship is there, but nothing to center a whole essay on.

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE-01 » Fri Jul 11, 2014 6:45 am Just one question regarding the Yui, Kaworu, Kaji and Mari relation... Was it ever implied that Yui was going to die during flashback episodes? Also, has Mari been implied in 2.0 or Q to be going in that direction?

[]:             Quote Post by obliterates » Fri Jul 11, 2014 7:58 am goodnight humanitarian wrote: I think this analysis makes really good points but I don't get why it had to be framed in the context of James Bond. Kaji is less of a specific deconstruction of James Bond and more of a general subversion of traditional masculinity and, along with Misato, gender relations. The James Bond relationship is there, but nothing to center a whole essay on.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sun Jul 13, 2014 1:08 pm Does everthing always have to have to do with gender?

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Jul 13, 2014 1:24 pm Kendrix wrote: Does everthing always have to have to do with gender?

[]:             Quote Post by Kvanu » Thu Jul 24, 2014 10:06 am Jump to Episode 17 for a moment. Kaji downs his first can of coffee and chucks it at the trashcan — creating a complete mess in the process — before tapping into “James Bond” mode with the Maya-Kaji sequence. Misato enters and, during a shot that many regard as puzzling, Kaji neatly organizes the coffee cans in the trash right before switching into his “sincere” side. Like I said, frames are never wasted in Evangelion. It’s a visual motif. The cluttered coffee cans signify when Kaji’s about to amplifying his front; similarly, the cans are organized when he decides to turn it off. In the very same episode, Shinji remarks that Misato likes “neat guys” when Toji refuses to help clean Rei’s room. Toji’s justification? It’s not the “manly" thing to do.

[]:             Quote Post by KatsuragiSansa » Tue Nov 04, 2014 10:12 pm Kvanu wrote: I dunno, but for me this is a clue that Kaji hasn't slept with anyone since Misato, just like Misato hasn't slept with anyone since Kaji.

[]:             Quote Post by Kvanu » Sat Nov 08, 2014 4:18 pm KatsuragiSansa wrote: What makes you deduce that from it? I'm not antagonizing you, just trying to understand your line of thought because it poked my curiosity.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sat Nov 08, 2014 5:13 pm Watching that scene again, I think people are perhaps reading too much into it. Shortly before that scene we get the bit with Shinji cleaning Rei's apartment, where we learn that Misato doesn't like sloppy guys. So, Misato enters the break room and finds a mess, and Kaji wants to get on her good side. Cleaning up the mess is a minor yet simple way to do that. I don't really think it says much about his character beyond that. As to Misato's line about his love life? Well, he was just hitting on Maya, so I'm not sure why that's puzzling, either. She was just keeping it professional, since she was looking for answers. That said, I think the notion that he hasn't been with anyone apart from her is interesting, both because it's a subversion of our expectations given his demeanor and because it makes for a nice parallel with Misato, who likewise hasn't been with anyone but him. I don't know that there's any evidence for that take in the show, but I can't think of anything that suggests otherwise, either.

[]:             Quote Post by KatsuragiSansa » Sat Nov 08, 2014 9:16 pm Kvanu wrote: Because right as Misato says "I don't know anything about your private life " Kaji sorts out the coffee cans. Clean coffee cans = private life = clean private life. I dunno, but its plausible.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sat Nov 08, 2014 9:26 pm KatsuragiSansa wrote: Yes, maybe we're reading too much into that scene, but it's too random to be put there just because. The hubby and I are currently rewatching NGE/Director's cut with a more analytic eye to try to get a fresh read on the show after a couple of years, but we still haven't gotten that far in the series, so I'll pay extra attention when we come across that scene ;)

[]:             Quote Post by SEELE » Sat Nov 08, 2014 9:35 pm Bagheera wrote: I don't think it was put there just because; I definitely think it served a purpose, I just think that purpose is more straightforward than the post linked in the OP supposes. IMO He's saying "I understand you/I'm the guy for you/I'm sucking up to you", not "this is something quintessential about my character."
